title: Integrated molecular interpretation of NPM1-mutated AML with FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A
learning_objectives: Recognise the prognostic and therapeutic relevance of FLT3-ITD.
Understand the role of DNMT3A as a common cooperating mutation.
Separate AML diagnostic classification from molecular risk refinement.
clinical_vignette: A 51-year-old man presents with fatigue, mouth ulcers and gum bleeding. He has no previous history of myelodysplasia or chemotherapy exposure. Bone marrow examination confirms acute myeloid leukaemia. A rapid molecular panel and NGS are performed.

morphology.peripheral_blood: Numerous circulating blasts with occasional cup-like nuclear invaginations. No abnormal promyelocyte predominance.
morphology.bone_marrow: Hypercellular marrow replaced by blasts. Blasts show moderate cytoplasm and folded or cup-like nuclei. No significant multilineage dysplasia is identified.

flow_cytometry.interpretation: The immunophenotype supports AML and is compatible with an NPM1-mutated pattern, including absent CD34 and reduced HLA-DR.

cytogenetics.interpretation: Normal conventional karyotype. No recurrent AML-defining rearrangement detected on rapid FISH panel.

integrated_laboratory_diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukaemia with NPM1 mutation, with concurrent FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A mutation.
question: Which statement best reflects the integrated molecular interpretation of this case?
options: A. DNMT3A mutation supersedes NPM1 and reclassifies the case as AML with myelodysplasia-related gene mutation.
B. This is AML with NPM1 mutation; FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A are important co-mutations that influence risk assessment and management but do not replace the diagnostic entity.
C. The normal karyotype means this AML cannot be genetically classified.
D. FLT3-ITD alone defines the AML subtype, regardless of NPM1 status.
E. Reduced HLA-DR and absent CD34 are sufficient to diagnose acute promyelocytic leukaemia.
correct_answer: B. This is AML with NPM1 mutation; FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A are important co-mutations that influence risk assessment and management but do not replace the diagnostic entity.
explanation: This case is best classified as AML with NPM1 mutation. NPM1 mutation defines the diagnostic entity. Concurrent FLT3-ITD has major prognostic and therapeutic implications, including consideration of FLT3-targeted therapy according to local protocols and current recommendations. DNMT3A is a common cooperating mutation in NPM1-mutated AML and may reflect clonal architecture, but it does not supersede the NPM1-defined entity. Normal karyotype does not exclude genetically defined AML because many defining lesions are molecular rather than cytogenetic.

why_other_options_are_wrong.A: DNMT3A is not one of the defining myelodysplasia-related gene mutations used to reclassify AML as AML with myelodysplasia-related gene mutation in the same way as genes such as ASXL1, RUNX1, BCOR, EZH2, SF3B1, SRSF2, STAG2, U2AF1 or ZRSR2.
why_other_options_are_wrong.C: Many AML cases with normal karyotype are genetically defined by molecular lesions such as NPM1 mutation.
why_other_options_are_wrong.D: FLT3-ITD is clinically important but is not the defining diagnostic entity in this case.
why_other_options_are_wrong.E: Reduced HLA-DR and absent CD34 may be seen in NPM1-mutated AML and are not sufficient for APL; APL requires compatible morphology and PML::RARA or variant RARA rearrangement.

frcpath_pearl: When interpreting NGS, ask three separate questions: What defines the diagnosis? What modifies risk? What is therapeutically targetable?
common_pitfalls: Assuming DNMT3A mutation automatically means AML with myelodysplasia-related gene mutation.
Treating FLT3-ITD as the diagnostic entity rather than a prognostic and therapeutic marker.
Assuming normal karyotype excludes genetically defined AML.
Mistaking reduced HLA-DR and absent CD34 for APL without PML::RARA evidence.
take_home_message: NPM1 mutation defines the AML entity, while FLT3-ITD and DNMT3A refine risk, clonal interpretation and treatment planning.
references: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours, 5th Edition
